texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Small pilot test results from a Polaris TM membrane 1 MWe CO2 capture system
texts.text: Small pilot test results from a Polaris TM membrane 1 MWe CO2 capture system

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Jay Kniep a *, Richard Baker a , Carlos Casillas a , Ken Chan a , Ivy Huang a , Tim Merkel a , Vincent Nguyen a , Zhen Sun a , Xuezhen Wang a a Membrane Technology and Research, Inc., 39630 Eureka Dr., Newark, CA 94560, USA
texts.text: Jay Kniep a *, Richard Baker a , Carlos Casillas a , Ken Chan a , Ivy Huang a , Tim Merkel a , Vincent Nguyen a , Zhen Sun a , Xuezhen Wang a a Membrane Technology and Research, Inc., 39630 Eureka Dr., Newark, CA 94560, USA

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To mitigate the harmful effects of global climate change, CO2 in combustion sources must be captured and either sequestered or utilized.  Membrane technology is an attractive CO2 capture option because of advantages such as energy-efficient  passive  operation,  small  footprint,  no  hazardous  chemical  emissions,  handling  or  storage  issues, near instantaneous response, no steam use, and reduced water requirements.
texts.text: To mitigate the harmful effects of global climate change, CO2 in combustion sources must be captured and either sequestered or utilized.  Membrane technology is an attractive CO2 capture option because of advantages such as energy-efficient  passive  operation,  small  footprint,  no  hazardous  chemical  emissions,  handling  or  storage  issues, near instantaneous response, no steam use, and reduced water requirements.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Working  with  the  U.S.  Department  of  Energy  (DOE),  Membrane  Technology  and  Research,  Inc.  (MTR)  has developed  new  membranes  and  process  designs  to  recover  CO2  from  combustion  gases.    The  Polaris™  class  of membranes offer the highest combination of CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity of any commercial membrane today.  These membranes are combined with a novel two-step process design that uses incoming combustion air to sweep  membranes  and  recycle  CO2  to  the  combustion  process.    The  benefits  of  this  two-step  membrane  design include an increased CO2 concentration to the membrane capture step and a reduction in the fraction of CO2 removal required by the capture step.  Design calculations estimate that this membrane process can capture CO2 at a cost of <$40/tonne under partial capture conditions.
texts.text: Working  with  the  U.S.  Department  of  Energy  (DOE),  Membrane  Technology  and  Research,  Inc.  (MTR)  has developed  new  membranes  and  process  designs  to  recover  CO2  from  combustion  gases.    The  Polaris™  class  of membranes offer the highest combination of CO2 permeance and CO2/N2 selectivity of any commercial membrane today.  These membranes are combined with a novel two-step process design that uses incoming combustion air to sweep  membranes  and  recycle  CO2  to  the  combustion  process.    The  benefits  of  this  two-step  membrane  design include an increased CO2 concentration to the membrane capture step and a reduction in the fraction of CO2 removal required by the capture step.  Design calculations estimate that this membrane process can capture CO2 at a cost of <$40/tonne under partial capture conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Between  2012  and  2015,  MTR  operated  a  bench-scale  test  system  at  the  U.S.  National  Carbon  Capture  Center (NCCC)  in  Wilsonville,  AL  testing  commercial-scale  Polaris  membrane  modules  capturing  1  tonne  CO2/day. Overall, this system accumulated a run time of over 11,000 hours, and demonstrated stable membrane performance while  processing  real  coal-fired  flue  gas.    The  test  system  also  operated  as  a  field  laboratory  that  allowed  for evaluation of new membrane module designs and validation of advanced membranes under real world conditions. With  this  experience,  MTR  designed  and  built  a  scaled-up  small  pilot  unit  to  treat  20  tonnes  CO 2 /day  from  a slipstream  at  NCCC  (equivalent  to  1  MWe-scale  coal-fired  power  generation).  This  system  was  operated  for approximately 1,500 hours in 2015 over two different test campaigns.  The small pilot membrane system exhibited stable  performance  and  reached  up  to  a  90%  capture  rate  during  operation  in  both  winter  and  summer  field  test conditions.    During  the  second  test  campaign,  advanced  Polaris  sweep  modules  demonstrating  higher  packing density, lower pressure drop, and higher permeance were validated.
texts.text: Between  2012  and  2015,  MTR  operated  a  bench-scale  test  system  at  the  U.S.  National  Carbon  Capture  Center (NCCC)  in  Wilsonville,  AL  testing  commercial-scale  Polaris  membrane  modules  capturing  1  tonne  CO2/day. Overall, this system accumulated a run time of over 11,000 hours, and demonstrated stable membrane performance while  processing  real  coal-fired  flue  gas.    The  test  system  also  operated  as  a  field  laboratory  that  allowed  for evaluation of new membrane module designs and validation of advanced membranes under real world conditions. With  this  experience,  MTR  designed  and  built  a  scaled-up  small  pilot  unit  to  treat  20  tonnes  CO 2 /day  from  a slipstream  at  NCCC  (equivalent  to  1  MWe-scale  coal-fired  power  generation).  This  system  was  operated  for approximately 1,500 hours in 2015 over two different test campaigns.  The small pilot membrane system exhibited stable  performance  and  reached  up  to  a  90%  capture  rate  during  operation  in  both  winter  and  summer  field  test conditions.    During  the  second  test  campaign,  advanced  Polaris  sweep  modules  demonstrating  higher  packing density, lower pressure drop, and higher permeance were validated.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After completion of testing at NCCC, the 20 tonne CO2/day small pilot membrane unit was moved to Babcock & Wilcox's (B&W) boiler research facility in Barberton, OH for a fully integrated recycle test of the membrane unit with an appropriately sized coal-fired boiler.  Previous CFD modeling and operation of the B&W research boiler with  CO2-laden  air  indicated  that  boiler  operation  with  recycled  CO 2 is  feasible  as  a  retrofit  with  no  necessary burner  modifications  and  only  a  minor  net  reduction  in  plant  efficiency.    The  field  test  conducted  in  fall  2016 demonstrated boiler operation with CO2-laden air recycled by a membrane system for the first time.  Parametric tests at B&W utilizing natural gas and two different coals validated simulated system performance.
texts.text: After completion of testing at NCCC, the 20 tonne CO2/day small pilot membrane unit was moved to Babcock & Wilcox's (B&W) boiler research facility in Barberton, OH for a fully integrated recycle test of the membrane unit with an appropriately sized coal-fired boiler.  Previous CFD modeling and operation of the B&W research boiler with  CO2-laden  air  indicated  that  boiler  operation  with  recycled  CO 2 is  feasible  as  a  retrofit  with  no  necessary burner  modifications  and  only  a  minor  net  reduction  in  plant  efficiency.    The  field  test  conducted  in  fall  2016 demonstrated boiler operation with CO2-laden air recycled by a membrane system for the first time.  Parametric tests at B&W utilizing natural gas and two different coals validated simulated system performance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Keywords: CO2 capture; power plant; membrane; process design; sweep module
texts.text: Keywords: CO2 capture; power plant; membrane; process design; sweep module

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Over the past decade, DOE has funded a large research effort to identify low-cost ways to capture CO2 from the emissions of large point sources, such as power generation facilities, to mitigate the climate impact of unabated CO2 emissions.    Coal-fired  power  plants  have  been  a  particular  focus  for  CO 2 capture  efforts  because  of  the  large installed base of these plants, which produce almost 40% of U.S. CO2 emissions.  Moreover, the relative low cost and large supply of coal suggests that this fuel will remain important to power production for the foreseeable future [1,2].
texts.text: Over the past decade, DOE has funded a large research effort to identify low-cost ways to capture CO2 from the emissions of large point sources, such as power generation facilities, to mitigate the climate impact of unabated CO2 emissions.    Coal-fired  power  plants  have  been  a  particular  focus  for  CO 2 capture  efforts  because  of  the  large installed base of these plants, which produce almost 40% of U.S. CO2 emissions.  Moreover, the relative low cost and large supply of coal suggests that this fuel will remain important to power production for the foreseeable future [1,2].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Currently, amine absorption is the leading candidate technology for post-combustion CO2 capture.  This capture approach is a proven technology used successfully to remove CO2 from industrial gas streams for decades.  Initial capture systems at commercial power stations such as Boundary Dam in Canada and WA Parish in Texas use amine absorption.  However, studies indicate that amine absorption, when applied to flue gas CO2 capture, is costly and energy  intensive  [2,3].    With  estimated  capture  costs  of  >$60/tonne  CO 2 ,  the  economics  of  carbon  capture  are unattractive  even  in  cases  where  the  captured  CO 2 can  be  utilized.    As  a  result,  DOE  is  funding  development  of transformative new technologies based on advanced solvents, membranes, or hybrids with a goal of reducing capture costs to $30/tonne or less.
texts.text: Currently, amine absorption is the leading candidate technology for post-combustion CO2 capture.  This capture approach is a proven technology used successfully to remove CO2 from industrial gas streams for decades.  Initial capture systems at commercial power stations such as Boundary Dam in Canada and WA Parish in Texas use amine absorption.  However, studies indicate that amine absorption, when applied to flue gas CO2 capture, is costly and energy  intensive  [2,3].    With  estimated  capture  costs  of  >$60/tonne  CO 2 ,  the  economics  of  carbon  capture  are unattractive  even  in  cases  where  the  captured  CO 2 can  be  utilized.    As  a  result,  DOE  is  funding  development  of transformative new technologies based on advanced solvents, membranes, or hybrids with a goal of reducing capture costs to $30/tonne or less.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Among advanced capture technologies being considered are a number of membrane approaches [4-7].  Membrane processes  offer  some  advantages  when  applied  to  post-combustion  CO2  capture  including  no  hazardous  chemical storage, handling or emissions issues, simple passive operation, tolerance to high SOx and NOx content, recovery of flue gas water, near instantaneous response times, no modifications to the existing power plant steam cycle (because they use only electric power), and very efficient partial capture.  The main challenge for post-combustion capture membranes is the low partial pressure of CO2 in flue gas, which results in very large membrane area being required because  of  the  small  driving  force  for  separation.    Some  years  ago,  working  with  DOE,  MTR  made  two transformative innovations to address this problem:
texts.text: Among advanced capture technologies being considered are a number of membrane approaches [4-7].  Membrane processes  offer  some  advantages  when  applied  to  post-combustion  CO2  capture  including  no  hazardous  chemical storage, handling or emissions issues, simple passive operation, tolerance to high SOx and NOx content, recovery of flue gas water, near instantaneous response times, no modifications to the existing power plant steam cycle (because they use only electric power), and very efficient partial capture.  The main challenge for post-combustion capture membranes is the low partial pressure of CO2 in flue gas, which results in very large membrane area being required because  of  the  small  driving  force  for  separation.    Some  years  ago,  working  with  DOE,  MTR  made  two transformative innovations to address this problem:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · A new class of high permeance membranes, called Polaris™, was developed.  This membrane was approximately tenfold more permeable than prior commercial membranes, resulting in a large decrease in required membrane area, and thus capital cost.
texts.text: A new class of high permeance membranes, called Polaris™, was developed.  This membrane was approximately tenfold more permeable than prior commercial membranes, resulting in a large decrease in required membrane area, and thus capital cost.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · A membrane selective recycle process was developed.  This patented process uses combustion air as a sweep stream  to  generate  driving  force  for  transmembrane  CO2 transport  [8].    The  separated  CO 2 is  recycled  to  the boiler with air.  This design increases the concentration of CO2 in flue gas, which reduces the energy and capital required for capture.
texts.text: A membrane selective recycle process was developed.  This patented process uses combustion air as a sweep stream  to  generate  driving  force  for  transmembrane  CO2 transport  [8].    The  separated  CO 2 is  recycled  to  the boiler with air.  This design increases the concentration of CO2 in flue gas, which reduces the energy and capital required for capture.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Polaris  is  a  composite  membrane  developed  by  MTR  with  a  selective  layer  based  on polar polymers  that  are extremely  permeable  to  CO2  and  other  polar  species.    The  first  generation  (Gen-1)  Polaris  membrane  set  the standard  against  which  all  post-combustion  capture  membranes  are  now  compared.    With  an  average  CO2 permeance of 1,000 gpu and a CO2/N2 pure-gas selectivity of 50, Gen-1 Polaris was a step-change improvement
texts.text: Polaris  is  a  composite  membrane  developed  by  MTR  with  a  selective  layer  based  on polar polymers  that  are extremely  permeable  to  CO2  and  other  polar  species.    The  first  generation  (Gen-1)  Polaris  membrane  set  the standard  against  which  all  post-combustion  capture  membranes  are  now  compared.    With  an  average  CO2 permeance of 1,000 gpu and a CO2/N2 pure-gas selectivity of 50, Gen-1 Polaris was a step-change improvement

texts.label: text
texts.orig: over typical commercial CO2-selective membranes used for natural gas treatment (which offer a CO2 permeance of around 100 gpu combined with a pure-gas CO2/N2 selectivity of 30).  This improvement is illustrated in Figure 1, where  membrane  performance  is  compared  in  the  form  of  a  trade-off  plot  of  CO2/N2  selectivity  versus  CO2 permeance.  Better membranes will have properties that move up and to the right on this plot.
texts.text: over typical commercial CO2-selective membranes used for natural gas treatment (which offer a CO2 permeance of around 100 gpu combined with a pure-gas CO2/N2 selectivity of 30).  This improvement is illustrated in Figure 1, where  membrane  performance  is  compared  in  the  form  of  a  trade-off  plot  of  CO2/N2  selectivity  versus  CO2 permeance.  Better membranes will have properties that move up and to the right on this plot.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure  1.    A  CO2/N2  trade-off  plot  showing  data  for  several  generations  of  MTR  Polaris  membrane.    Data  are  pure-gas  values  at  room temperature with a feed pressure of 50 psig and permeate pressure of 0 psig.
texts.text: Figure  1.    A  CO2/N2  trade-off  plot  showing  data  for  several  generations  of  MTR  Polaris  membrane.    Data  are  pure-gas  values  at  room temperature with a feed pressure of 50 psig and permeate pressure of 0 psig.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to showcasing the benefits of Polaris over conventional membranes, Figure 1 also shows some of the more recent improvements in the performance of these membranes.  A second generation (Gen-2) Polaris membrane has  been  scaled  up  to  pilot  production.    This  membrane  offers  about  double  the  CO2  permeance  with  similar selectivity  to  the  base  case  Polaris.    These  Gen-2  membranes  have  been  fabricated  into  prototype  modules,  and validated in bench-scale testing at NCCC.  Recently, even higher permeance Gen-3 Polaris membranes (3,000 gpu) have  been  produced  at  the  lab  scale.    These  improvements  are  important  because  the  size  and  capital  cost  of  a membrane unit scales almost linearly with membrane CO2 permeance.  Thus, these new Polaris membranes would yield  a  system  with  one-half  to  one-third  as  many  membrane  vessels  as  the  Gen-1  membranes;  this  would  be  a dramatic reduction in system size and cost.
texts.text: In addition to showcasing the benefits of Polaris over conventional membranes, Figure 1 also shows some of the more recent improvements in the performance of these membranes.  A second generation (Gen-2) Polaris membrane has  been  scaled  up  to  pilot  production.    This  membrane  offers  about  double  the  CO2  permeance  with  similar selectivity  to  the  base  case  Polaris.    These  Gen-2  membranes  have  been  fabricated  into  prototype  modules,  and validated in bench-scale testing at NCCC.  Recently, even higher permeance Gen-3 Polaris membranes (3,000 gpu) have  been  produced  at  the  lab  scale.    These  improvements  are  important  because  the  size  and  capital  cost  of  a membrane unit scales almost linearly with membrane CO2 permeance.  Thus, these new Polaris membranes would yield  a  system  with  one-half  to  one-third  as  many  membrane  vessels  as  the  Gen-1  membranes;  this  would  be  a dramatic reduction in system size and cost.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In addition to a membrane with good separation performance, an energy-efficient and affordable process design is required to make membranes competitive for post-combustion CO2 capture.  To overcome inherent driving force issues and achieve a relatively high CO2 capture rate and high purity, membrane developers have proposed multistep/stage membrane designs and/or hybrids with other separation technologies (cryogenics, absorption, etc.).  For example,  MTR  developed  the  selective  recycle  process  design  shown  in  Figure  2  [8].    This  process  uses  a combination of a small amount of feed compression and permeate vacuum in a first membrane step to efficiently generate a pressure ratio that will lead to capture of about 50% of the inlet flue gas CO2.  The partially-treated flue gas  leaving  this  primary  CO 2 removal  unit  is  then  sent  to  a  second  membrane  step  that  utilizes  a  sweep  gas  of combustion air to selectively recycle CO2 to the boiler and drive the overall CO2 recovery up to 90%.
texts.text: In addition to a membrane with good separation performance, an energy-efficient and affordable process design is required to make membranes competitive for post-combustion CO2 capture.  To overcome inherent driving force issues and achieve a relatively high CO2 capture rate and high purity, membrane developers have proposed multistep/stage membrane designs and/or hybrids with other separation technologies (cryogenics, absorption, etc.).  For example,  MTR  developed  the  selective  recycle  process  design  shown  in  Figure  2  [8].    This  process  uses  a combination of a small amount of feed compression and permeate vacuum in a first membrane step to efficiently generate a pressure ratio that will lead to capture of about 50% of the inlet flue gas CO2.  The partially-treated flue gas  leaving  this  primary  CO 2 removal  unit  is  then  sent  to  a  second  membrane  step  that  utilizes  a  sweep  gas  of combustion air to selectively recycle CO2 to the boiler and drive the overall CO2 recovery up to 90%.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 2. Simplified diagram of the MTR selective recycle CO2 capture process at a coal-fired power plant.
texts.text: Figure 2. Simplified diagram of the MTR selective recycle CO2 capture process at a coal-fired power plant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This CO2 recycle design has a number of features that optimize membrane system performance:
texts.text: This CO2 recycle design has a number of features that optimize membrane system performance:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Because it  is  a  two-step  membrane  design,  all  of  the  flue  gas  CO 2 does  not  have  to  be  removed  in  a  single membrane step.  This allows the first-step membrane to operate efficiently as a partial capture step (~50%) with a  relatively  high  partial  pressure  of  CO 2 on  the  feed-side.    Because  membranes  rely  on  partial  pressure difference to drive permeation, they perform more effectively with higher feed CO2 concentration.
texts.text: Because it  is  a  two-step  membrane  design,  all  of  the  flue  gas  CO 2 does  not  have  to  be  removed  in  a  single membrane step.  This allows the first-step membrane to operate efficiently as a partial capture step (~50%) with a  relatively  high  partial  pressure  of  CO 2 on  the  feed-side.    Because  membranes  rely  on  partial  pressure difference to drive permeation, they perform more effectively with higher feed CO2 concentration.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The second membrane step performs the difficult task of removing CO2 to very low levels (i.e., to reach 90% capture).  This step uses an air sweep stream to maintain separation driving force by keeping a relatively low partial pressure of CO2 on the permeate-side.  Because the air stream is already being blown into the boiler as the  oxidant  for  combustion,  this  sweep  gas  provides  an  essentially  free  separation  (i.e.,  no  compressors  or vacuum pumps are used in this step).
texts.text: The second membrane step performs the difficult task of removing CO2 to very low levels (i.e., to reach 90% capture).  This step uses an air sweep stream to maintain separation driving force by keeping a relatively low partial pressure of CO2 on the permeate-side.  Because the air stream is already being blown into the boiler as the  oxidant  for  combustion,  this  sweep  gas  provides  an  essentially  free  separation  (i.e.,  no  compressors  or vacuum pumps are used in this step).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · The concentration of CO2 in the flue gas leaving the boiler is increased (for example, from 12% to 20% CO2) because CO2 is recycled to the boiler with the air sweep stream.  This enrichment makes CO2 capture in the first membrane step easier due to the higher CO2 partial pressure in the feed.
texts.text: The concentration of CO2 in the flue gas leaving the boiler is increased (for example, from 12% to 20% CO2) because CO2 is recycled to the boiler with the air sweep stream.  This enrichment makes CO2 capture in the first membrane step easier due to the higher CO2 partial pressure in the feed.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Subsequently, MTR has worked with DOE to develop these membrane and process design innovations into a cost-effective commercial CO2 capture process.  This effort has included the first test of membrane modules with coal-fired flue gas at the Arizona Public Services (APS) Cholla plant in 2010; the accumulation of >11,000 hours of flue  gas  operation  for  Polaris  modules  on  a  bench-scale  1  tonne/day  (TPD)  system  at  NCCC;  scale-up  of  first generation Polaris to a 20 TPD small pilot system, and successful operation of this system on a flue gas slipstream at NCCC and in integrated boiler testing at B&W.
texts.text: Subsequently, MTR has worked with DOE to develop these membrane and process design innovations into a cost-effective commercial CO2 capture process.  This effort has included the first test of membrane modules with coal-fired flue gas at the Arizona Public Services (APS) Cholla plant in 2010; the accumulation of >11,000 hours of flue  gas  operation  for  Polaris  modules  on  a  bench-scale  1  tonne/day  (TPD)  system  at  NCCC;  scale-up  of  first generation Polaris to a 20 TPD small pilot system, and successful operation of this system on a flue gas slipstream at NCCC and in integrated boiler testing at B&W.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Starting in 2012, MTR operated a 1 TPD bench-scale capture system processing a slipstream of coal-fired flue gas from an existing power plant at NCCC.  This system accumulated over 11,000 hours of operation with flue gas testing spiral-wound modules in both steps of the MTR process: a capture step operating with permeate vacuum and a selective recycle step that uses air sweep to provide driving force for CO2 removal.  The system was a valuable field  laboratory  that  provided  important  membrane  lifetime  data  under  real  world  conditions,  validation  of membrane performance improvements, and balance of plant operational lessons useful for future scale-up [9,10].
texts.text: Starting in 2012, MTR operated a 1 TPD bench-scale capture system processing a slipstream of coal-fired flue gas from an existing power plant at NCCC.  This system accumulated over 11,000 hours of operation with flue gas testing spiral-wound modules in both steps of the MTR process: a capture step operating with permeate vacuum and a selective recycle step that uses air sweep to provide driving force for CO2 removal.  The system was a valuable field  laboratory  that  provided  important  membrane  lifetime  data  under  real  world  conditions,  validation  of membrane performance improvements, and balance of plant operational lessons useful for future scale-up [9,10].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 3(a) shows an example of performance data obtained on the 1 TPD system during a NCCC campaign. Over the course of more than 500 hours, the system consistently captured over 90% of the inlet CO2.  The lack of any performance degradation in these tests confirms that the membrane modules are stable in the presence of coal flue gas.  This type of information is important to estimate how often modules will need to be changed in future commercial  systems.    Current  economic  projections  are  based  on  a  3-year  module  lifetime.    Considering  the cumulative stable operating time of more than 1 year on the 1 TPD system at NCCC, this estimate seems reasonable. MTR's commercial membrane systems deployed at industrial sites routinely last 3 to 5 years, in some cases twice this amount.
texts.text: Figure 3(a) shows an example of performance data obtained on the 1 TPD system during a NCCC campaign. Over the course of more than 500 hours, the system consistently captured over 90% of the inlet CO2.  The lack of any performance degradation in these tests confirms that the membrane modules are stable in the presence of coal flue gas.  This type of information is important to estimate how often modules will need to be changed in future commercial  systems.    Current  economic  projections  are  based  on  a  3-year  module  lifetime.    Considering  the cumulative stable operating time of more than 1 year on the 1 TPD system at NCCC, this estimate seems reasonable. MTR's commercial membrane systems deployed at industrial sites routinely last 3 to 5 years, in some cases twice this amount.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure  3(b)  shows  an  example  of  another  type  of  useful  information  provided  by  the  1  TPD  system:  new membrane  performance  validation.    As  previously  described,  the  higher  CO2  permeance  of  Gen-2  Polaris membranes will reduce membrane equipment size, and is an important step toward meeting future DOE cost targets. To insure that lab improvements translate to the field, modules were made using the Gen-2 membrane and tested side-by-side with Gen-1 membrane modules on the 1 TPD system.  The bench-scale test results shown in Figure 3(b) confirm that the Gen-2 membrane modules consistently remove roughly double the amount of CO2 compared to the Gen-1 membrane.
texts.text: Figure  3(b)  shows  an  example  of  another  type  of  useful  information  provided  by  the  1  TPD  system:  new membrane  performance  validation.    As  previously  described,  the  higher  CO2  permeance  of  Gen-2  Polaris membranes will reduce membrane equipment size, and is an important step toward meeting future DOE cost targets. To insure that lab improvements translate to the field, modules were made using the Gen-2 membrane and tested side-by-side with Gen-1 membrane modules on the 1 TPD system.  The bench-scale test results shown in Figure 3(b) confirm that the Gen-2 membrane modules consistently remove roughly double the amount of CO2 compared to the Gen-1 membrane.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 3. (a) CO2 capture rate for the 1 TPD MTR system at NCCC as a function of operating time, and (b) a comparison of CO2 removal by Gen-1 and Gen-2 Polaris modules.
texts.text: Figure 3. (a) CO2 capture rate for the 1 TPD MTR system at NCCC as a function of operating time, and (b) a comparison of CO2 removal by Gen-1 and Gen-2 Polaris modules.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Using the field test performance data and experience gained from operating the 1 TPD system, MTR designed and built a scaled-up small pilot unit to treat 20 tonnes CO2/day (20 TPD) from a slipstream at NCCC (equivalent to 1 MWe of coal-fired power generation).  This small pilot membrane system exhibited stable performance of Gen-1 Polaris modules over a 6 month period consisting of two test campaigns of operation in both winter and summer conditions.  During this test campaign, the system averaged 87% CO2 capture.  Figure 4(a) shows a picture of the MTR small pilot system at NCCC.  Also shown in Figure 4(b) for comparison are the 10 TPD pilot solvent test unit (PSTU) and a 20 TPD advanced amine system at NCCC.  This picture illustrates the compact nature of the MTR membrane system compared to other separation technologies.
texts.text: Using the field test performance data and experience gained from operating the 1 TPD system, MTR designed and built a scaled-up small pilot unit to treat 20 tonnes CO2/day (20 TPD) from a slipstream at NCCC (equivalent to 1 MWe of coal-fired power generation).  This small pilot membrane system exhibited stable performance of Gen-1 Polaris modules over a 6 month period consisting of two test campaigns of operation in both winter and summer conditions.  During this test campaign, the system averaged 87% CO2 capture.  Figure 4(a) shows a picture of the MTR small pilot system at NCCC.  Also shown in Figure 4(b) for comparison are the 10 TPD pilot solvent test unit (PSTU) and a 20 TPD advanced amine system at NCCC.  This picture illustrates the compact nature of the MTR membrane system compared to other separation technologies.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 4. Photos of (a) the MTR 20 TPD membrane system installed at NCCC and (b) the full NCCC test site showing the MTR system in comparison to two solvent capture systems.  Photos are courtesy of NCCC.
texts.text: Figure 4. Photos of (a) the MTR 20 TPD membrane system installed at NCCC and (b) the full NCCC test site showing the MTR system in comparison to two solvent capture systems.  Photos are courtesy of NCCC.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The 20 TPD system was also utilized to validate improvements in membrane module design.  One of the key issues for a membrane post-combustion capture system is how to balance the desire for a small system footprint with the  need  to  process  large  volumetric  flows  and  minimize  parasitic  pressure  drops.    The  pressure  drop  issue  is particularly  important  because  for  a  full-scale  (550  MW e power  plant)  membrane  capture  system,  each  1  psi  of pressure  drop  through  the  membrane  unit  amounts  to  approximately  3  MWe  of  required  blower  energy.    For previous techno-economic analyses (TEAs), MTR assumed 1.5 psi pressure drop through each of the  membrane steps shown in Figure 2 (i.e., this is the pressure drop from feed to residue in each of the two membrane steps, as well as the sweep-in to sweep-out pressure drop on the air sweep step).  The ability to reach this pressure drop target while  maintaining  a  compact  system  size  and  good  membrane  performance  depends  on  the  membrane  module design.
texts.text: The 20 TPD system was also utilized to validate improvements in membrane module design.  One of the key issues for a membrane post-combustion capture system is how to balance the desire for a small system footprint with the  need  to  process  large  volumetric  flows  and  minimize  parasitic  pressure  drops.    The  pressure  drop  issue  is particularly  important  because  for  a  full-scale  (550  MW e power  plant)  membrane  capture  system,  each  1  psi  of pressure  drop  through  the  membrane  unit  amounts  to  approximately  3  MWe  of  required  blower  energy.    For previous techno-economic analyses (TEAs), MTR assumed 1.5 psi pressure drop through each of the  membrane steps shown in Figure 2 (i.e., this is the pressure drop from feed to residue in each of the two membrane steps, as well as the sweep-in to sweep-out pressure drop on the air sweep step).  The ability to reach this pressure drop target while  maintaining  a  compact  system  size  and  good  membrane  performance  depends  on  the  membrane  module design.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In  earlier  work,  MTR  addressed  these  module  design  issues  by  first  adapting  existing  spiral-wound  module technology for low-pressure operation.  Later, recognizing some of the limitations associated with tortuous flow in spirals, new plate-and-frame modules were designed specifically for low pressure, high volume flue gas operation [11].  The most important feature of these new modules is the ability for fine control of the flow path on both the feed and sweep sides of the membrane, which can be used to minimize pressure drop.
texts.text: In  earlier  work,  MTR  addressed  these  module  design  issues  by  first  adapting  existing  spiral-wound  module technology for low-pressure operation.  Later, recognizing some of the limitations associated with tortuous flow in spirals, new plate-and-frame modules were designed specifically for low pressure, high volume flue gas operation [11].  The most important feature of these new modules is the ability for fine control of the flow path on both the feed and sweep sides of the membrane, which can be used to minimize pressure drop.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To validate laboratory findings, a prototype plate-and-frame module vessel was built and tested in a side-by-side comparison  with  spirals  on  the  small  pilot  system  at  NCCC.    Figure  5  shows  these  results,  which  confirm  the improved performance of the  new  modules.    At  the  same  flowrate,  the  plate-and-frame  module  had  about  3  psi lower  pressure  drop  compared  to  the  sweep  spirals.    Scaled  to  a  full  power  plant,  this  would  yield  savings  of approximately  10  MWe  in  fan  power.  These  energy  savings  from  reduced  pressure  drops  are  key  to  making membranes a competitive capture approach.
texts.text: To validate laboratory findings, a prototype plate-and-frame module vessel was built and tested in a side-by-side comparison  with  spirals  on  the  small  pilot  system  at  NCCC.    Figure  5  shows  these  results,  which  confirm  the improved performance of the  new  modules.    At  the  same  flowrate,  the  plate-and-frame  module  had  about  3  psi lower  pressure  drop  compared  to  the  sweep  spirals.    Scaled  to  a  full  power  plant,  this  would  yield  savings  of approximately  10  MWe  in  fan  power.  These  energy  savings  from  reduced  pressure  drops  are  key  to  making membranes a competitive capture approach.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 5. Difference in pressure drop between spiral and plate-and-frame modules in NCCC field tests.
texts.text: Figure 5. Difference in pressure drop between spiral and plate-and-frame modules in NCCC field tests.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: After testing at NCCC, the 20 TPD unit was decommissioned and shipped to the B&W Research Center in Ohio, where further trials were performed in an integrated fashion with recirculation of CO2 captured in the air sweep to an appropriately-sized coal boiler.  This program was different than the NCCC slipstream tests where recycle to the boiler  was  not  possible.    The  importance  of  the  B&W  field  tests  was  to  confirm  that  the  membrane  could  be operated with a boiler in an integrated manner and to quantify the impact of recycled CO2 on boiler performance. While prior computational fluid dynamics modeling and pilot testing of coal combustion with CO2-enriched air at B&W  had  concluded  that  CO2  recycle  using  sweep  membranes  appeared  feasible  as  a  retrofit,  testing  with  a membrane unit would be the ultimate validation of the recycle approach.
texts.text: After testing at NCCC, the 20 TPD unit was decommissioned and shipped to the B&W Research Center in Ohio, where further trials were performed in an integrated fashion with recirculation of CO2 captured in the air sweep to an appropriately-sized coal boiler.  This program was different than the NCCC slipstream tests where recycle to the boiler  was  not  possible.    The  importance  of  the  B&W  field  tests  was  to  confirm  that  the  membrane  could  be operated with a boiler in an integrated manner and to quantify the impact of recycled CO2 on boiler performance. While prior computational fluid dynamics modeling and pilot testing of coal combustion with CO2-enriched air at B&W  had  concluded  that  CO2  recycle  using  sweep  membranes  appeared  feasible  as  a  retrofit,  testing  with  a membrane unit would be the ultimate validation of the recycle approach.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A three month test campaign with the boiler operating on natural gas, Powder River Basin coal, and an Eastern Bituminius coal confirmed successful integrated operation of membrane and boiler.  During integrated CO2 recycle operation, stable combustion and boiler flames were observed over the entire boiler oxygen windbox content range examined (17 - 21%).  These gas compositions to the boiler correspond to different amounts of boiler secondary air being  used  as  the  membrane  sweep  air.    There  was  minimal  impact  of  CO2  recycle  on  combustion  pollutant formation  (CO,  SO2,  NOx).    Combustion  with  and  without  CO2  recycle  is  sufficiently  similar  that  no  burner modification are required to obtain stable combustion.  While there were differences in the boiler heat adsorption when  CO2  recycle  was  used,  it  should  be  possible  to  use  an  existing  boiler  for  firing  with  sweep  gas  without pressure parts modifications.
texts.text: A three month test campaign with the boiler operating on natural gas, Powder River Basin coal, and an Eastern Bituminius coal confirmed successful integrated operation of membrane and boiler.  During integrated CO2 recycle operation, stable combustion and boiler flames were observed over the entire boiler oxygen windbox content range examined (17 - 21%).  These gas compositions to the boiler correspond to different amounts of boiler secondary air being  used  as  the  membrane  sweep  air.    There  was  minimal  impact  of  CO2  recycle  on  combustion  pollutant formation  (CO,  SO2,  NOx).    Combustion  with  and  without  CO2  recycle  is  sufficiently  similar  that  no  burner modification are required to obtain stable combustion.  While there were differences in the boiler heat adsorption when  CO2  recycle  was  used,  it  should  be  possible  to  use  an  existing  boiler  for  firing  with  sweep  gas  without pressure parts modifications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A modest reduction in boiler efficiency due to recycled CO2 was measured and found to be consistent with that estimated from modeling and accounted for in prior techno-economic analyses (TEAs).  As the amount of secondary air used to sweep the membrane is increased, the boiler efficiency loss increases in a roughly linear manner.  By varying the amount of air used as sweep experimentally, the membrane system was able to achieve capture rates of 50  to  90%.    Figure  6(a)  shows  a  picture  of  the  small  pilot  system  installed  at  B&W  and  Figure  6(b)  shows  the influence of the sweep flow rate on overall measured capture rate.  The line in Figure 6(b) is a guide for the eye only and all data points were taken with a flue gas system pressure of 30 psia.  As the air flowrate to the sweep step membrane modules increases, permeate-side CO2 partial pressure is reduced, driving additional CO2 removal from the flue gas stream.  Consistent with design calculations, the membrane system captured ~65% of the CO2 without any air sweep.  Increasing the sweep air flow rate allowed the system to gradually increase the capture rate to 90%.
texts.text: A modest reduction in boiler efficiency due to recycled CO2 was measured and found to be consistent with that estimated from modeling and accounted for in prior techno-economic analyses (TEAs).  As the amount of secondary air used to sweep the membrane is increased, the boiler efficiency loss increases in a roughly linear manner.  By varying the amount of air used as sweep experimentally, the membrane system was able to achieve capture rates of 50  to  90%.    Figure  6(a)  shows  a  picture  of  the  small  pilot  system  installed  at  B&W  and  Figure  6(b)  shows  the influence of the sweep flow rate on overall measured capture rate.  The line in Figure 6(b) is a guide for the eye only and all data points were taken with a flue gas system pressure of 30 psia.  As the air flowrate to the sweep step membrane modules increases, permeate-side CO2 partial pressure is reduced, driving additional CO2 removal from the flue gas stream.  Consistent with design calculations, the membrane system captured ~65% of the CO2 without any air sweep.  Increasing the sweep air flow rate allowed the system to gradually increase the capture rate to 90%.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 6. (a) Picture of the 20 TPD test system installed at the B&W Research Center, and (b) Field test data showing capture rate as a function of air sweep flow rate.
texts.text: Figure 6. (a) Picture of the 20 TPD test system installed at the B&W Research Center, and (b) Field test data showing capture rate as a function of air sweep flow rate.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.  Techno-Economic Analysis of the MTR CO2 Capture Process
texts.text: 4.  Techno-Economic Analysis of the MTR CO2 Capture Process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Previously, a number of TEAs of the MTR membrane post-combustion capture process have been conducted. For example, a recent DOE TEA report (The Pathways Study) on future technologies for post-combustion carbon capture compares the MTR membrane approach favorably with various amine processes [12].  This study shows a membrane system using advanced Polaris membranes and efficient compression equipment capturing 90% of the CO2  from  an  ultra-supercritical  coal  plant  with  a  35%  increase  in  cost  of  electricity  (COE)  and  a  cost  of  CO 2 avoided of <$40/tonne.  While these results are promising, we believe relaxing the constraint on 90% capture makes membranes even more attractive as a capture approach.
texts.text: Previously, a number of TEAs of the MTR membrane post-combustion capture process have been conducted. For example, a recent DOE TEA report (The Pathways Study) on future technologies for post-combustion carbon capture compares the MTR membrane approach favorably with various amine processes [12].  This study shows a membrane system using advanced Polaris membranes and efficient compression equipment capturing 90% of the CO2  from  an  ultra-supercritical  coal  plant  with  a  35%  increase  in  cost  of  electricity  (COE)  and  a  cost  of  CO 2 avoided of <$40/tonne.  While these results are promising, we believe relaxing the constraint on 90% capture makes membranes even more attractive as a capture approach.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As part of a recent DOE-funded program, MTR completed a TEA that included an examination of the impact of membrane permeance and capture rate on costs.  Figure 7(a) shows that capture costs are a relatively strong function of membrane permeance with advanced membranes approaching $40/tonne at 90% capture.  Figure 7(b) shows that significantly lower costs are possible at lower capture rates.  For both of the membrane designs examined in this figure, the capture cost exhibits a minimum in the 30% to 60% capture range, with the lowest cost below $40/tonne. This  result  is  consistent  with  industrial  use  where  membranes  are  known  to  be  well  suited  for  bulk  separations. Such behavior is different than absorption processes, which show little change in capture cost below 90% capture. As a consequence,  membranes may be the most affordable  way to reduce coal plant  CO2 emissions to that of a natural  gas  plant  (which  requires  about  50%  capture  from  a  coal  plant).    Moreover,  they  may  be  a  good  fit  for utilization cases, such as enhanced oil recovery, where users are interested in CO2 at the lowest cost regardless of capture rate.
texts.text: As part of a recent DOE-funded program, MTR completed a TEA that included an examination of the impact of membrane permeance and capture rate on costs.  Figure 7(a) shows that capture costs are a relatively strong function of membrane permeance with advanced membranes approaching $40/tonne at 90% capture.  Figure 7(b) shows that significantly lower costs are possible at lower capture rates.  For both of the membrane designs examined in this figure, the capture cost exhibits a minimum in the 30% to 60% capture range, with the lowest cost below $40/tonne. This  result  is  consistent  with  industrial  use  where  membranes  are  known  to  be  well  suited  for  bulk  separations. Such behavior is different than absorption processes, which show little change in capture cost below 90% capture. As a consequence,  membranes may be the most affordable  way to reduce coal plant  CO2 emissions to that of a natural  gas  plant  (which  requires  about  50%  capture  from  a  coal  plant).    Moreover,  they  may  be  a  good  fit  for utilization cases, such as enhanced oil recovery, where users are interested in CO2 at the lowest cost regardless of capture rate.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 7 also compares the capture costs for two membrane designs: the two-step process with recycle described previously in Figure 2, and a one-step process where sweep recycle is not used.  Over most of the capture rate range, and  particularly  at  high  capture  rates,  the  Figure  2  design  shows  lower  costs.    This  difference  demonstrates  the ability of  the  sweep design to reduce cost particularly at high CO2 removal rates (by as much as $25/tonne).  At partial  capture  rates,  the  difference  in  cost  between  the  two  membrane  designs  is  smaller.    When  the  additional complexity associated with recycling CO2 to the boiler is considered, there may be partial capture cases where the simple one-step process without recycle is preferred.
texts.text: Figure 7 also compares the capture costs for two membrane designs: the two-step process with recycle described previously in Figure 2, and a one-step process where sweep recycle is not used.  Over most of the capture rate range, and  particularly  at  high  capture  rates,  the  Figure  2  design  shows  lower  costs.    This  difference  demonstrates  the ability of  the  sweep design to reduce cost particularly at high CO2 removal rates (by as much as $25/tonne).  At partial  capture  rates,  the  difference  in  cost  between  the  two  membrane  designs  is  smaller.    When  the  additional complexity associated with recycling CO2 to the boiler is considered, there may be partial capture cases where the simple one-step process without recycle is preferred.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 7. (a) Effect of membrane permeance on the cost of capture at 90% CO2 capture and (b) effect of capture rate on the capture cost for membrane process designs with and without sweep recycle.
texts.text: Figure 7. (a) Effect of membrane permeance on the cost of capture at 90% CO2 capture and (b) effect of capture rate on the capture cost for membrane process designs with and without sweep recycle.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: With support from the U.S. Department of Energy, MTR has worked to develop efficient membrane CO2 capture technology.  This effort has produced the Polaris class of membranes and a patented selective recycle process design that lowers the cost of capture.  This promising capture approach has been validated in bench-scale slipstream tests (0.05  MWe  or  1  tonne  CO2/day)  at  the  National  Carbon  Capture  Center  (NCCC)  in  Wilsonville,  AL  where  first generation Polaris modules accumulated ~11,000 hours of operation with coal-derived flue gas. In recent work, the Polaris capture system was scaled-up to a small pilot unit (1 MWe or 20 tonne CO2/day) and validated in testing at NCCC  and  a  Babcock  &  Wilcox  (B&W)  research  facility  in  Ohio.    Techno-economic  analyses  of  the  MTR membrane CO2 capture process are favorable when compared to alternative approaches and show the potential of the MTR process to reach <$40/tonne CO2 under partial capture conditions.
texts.text: With support from the U.S. Department of Energy, MTR has worked to develop efficient membrane CO2 capture technology.  This effort has produced the Polaris class of membranes and a patented selective recycle process design that lowers the cost of capture.  This promising capture approach has been validated in bench-scale slipstream tests (0.05  MWe  or  1  tonne  CO2/day)  at  the  National  Carbon  Capture  Center  (NCCC)  in  Wilsonville,  AL  where  first generation Polaris modules accumulated ~11,000 hours of operation with coal-derived flue gas. In recent work, the Polaris capture system was scaled-up to a small pilot unit (1 MWe or 20 tonne CO2/day) and validated in testing at NCCC  and  a  Babcock  &  Wilcox  (B&W)  research  facility  in  Ohio.    Techno-economic  analyses  of  the  MTR membrane CO2 capture process are favorable when compared to alternative approaches and show the potential of the MTR process to reach <$40/tonne CO2 under partial capture conditions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In future work, MTR will scale-up next generation Polaris membranes and modules to a final form optimized for commercial  use  and  validate  performance  in  an  engineering-scale  field  test  at  the  Technology  Centre  Mongstad (TCM) in Norway by 2021.
texts.text: In future work, MTR will scale-up next generation Polaris membranes and modules to a final form optimized for commercial  use  and  validate  performance  in  an  engineering-scale  field  test  at  the  Technology  Centre  Mongstad (TCM) in Norway by 2021.
